claim.content: The extrapolated thermodynamic-limit effective masses exhibit distinct density dependences depending on spin polarization: for the paramagnetic (unpolarized) 2D HEG over the density-parameter range \\(r_{s}=1\\) to 10 the extrapolated effective mass remains close to unity (\\(m^{*}\\approx1\\)), indicating no significant interaction-induced mass enhancement at these densities for the clean continuum 2D HEG model considered; by contrast, for the fully spin-polarized (ferromagnetic) 2D HEG the extrapolated effective mass decreases as the density is lowered, for example taking the values \\(0.85(1)\\) at \\(r_{s}=1\\), \\(0.74(1)\\) at \\(r_{s}=5\\), and \\(0.70(3)\\) at \\(r_{s}=10\\), a trend that is qualitatively consistent with experimental measurements on spin-polarized two-dimensional systems reported in the literature (e.g., Padmanabhan et al.).

conclusion.content: The extrapolated thermodynamic-limit effective masses exhibit distinct density dependences depending on spin polarization: for the paramagnetic (unpolarized) 2D HEG over the density-parameter range \\(r_{s}=1\\) to 10 the extrapolated effective mass remains close to unity (\\(m^{*}\\approx1\\)), indicating no significant interaction-induced mass enhancement at these densities for the clean continuum 2D HEG model considered; by contrast, for the fully spin-polarized (ferromagnetic) 2D HEG the extrapolated effective mass decreases as the density is lowered, for example taking the values \\(0.85(1)\\) at \\(r_{s}=1\\), \\(0.74(1)\\) at \\(r_{s}=5\\), and \\(0.70(3)\\) at \\(r_{s}=10\\), a trend that is qualitatively consistent with experimental measurements on spin-polarized two-dimensional systems reported in the literature (e.g., Padmanabhan et al.).

premises.content: For the paramagnetic (unpolarized) 2D HEG over the density-parameter range \\(r_{s}=1\\) to 10, let \\(m^{*}(\\infty)\\) denote the quasiparticle effective mass extrapolated to the thermodynamic limit by fitting the finite-size DMC effective masses to the single-power-law form \\(m^{*}(N)=m^{*}(\\infty)+b\\,N^{-\\gamma}\\) and adopting the exponent \\(\\gamma=3/2\\) for the extrapolation. The proposition is: the extrapolated numerical values \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=1)=0.955(2)\\), \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=5)=1.04(2)\\), and \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=10)=1.03(4)\\) indicate that the true thermodynamic-limit quasiparticle effective mass of the clean continuum-model paramagnetic 2D HEG remains close to unity across this \\(r_{s}\\) range and that there is no significant enhancement of \\(m^{*}\\) at low density for this physical system class.

Let the fully spin-polarized (ferromagnetic) 2D HEG be represented theoretically by the continuum-model DMC calculations and experimentally by high-quality semiconductor two-dimensional systems in which spin polarization can be induced (for example by applying an in-plane magnetic field). The proposition is: the suppression of the quasiparticle effective mass with decreasing density observed in the DMC-extrapolated ferromagnetic results (numerically \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=1)=0.851(5)\\), \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=5)=0.74(1)\\), and \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=10)=0.70(3)\\)) corresponds qualitatively to the trend measured in experiments cited in the literature (for example Padmanabhan et al.), and this qualitative agreement is not invalidated by differences in sample specifics, partial polarization, disorder, or experimental measurement methodology.

steps.reasoning: Treat the extrapolated thermodynamic-limit effective masses $m^{*}(\infty)$ for paramagnetic and ferromagnetic 2D HEGs (conclusion 5) as known inputs for drawing physical conclusions about density and spin-polarization dependence.

Compare the paramagnetic extrapolated values across the density range $r_{s}=1$--$10$: the reported values are $m^{*}(r_{s}=1)=0.955(2)$, $m^{*}(r_{s}=5)=1.04(2)$, and $m^{*}(r_{s}=10)=1.03(4)$, which are all close to unity; from these numerical results conclude that for the paramagnetic (unpolarized) 2D HEG in the considered range the quasiparticle effective mass remains close to $1$ and shows no significant enhancement at low density. 
Fig. 6

Compare the ferromagnetic extrapolated values across the same density range: the reported values are $m^{*}(r_{s}=1)=0.851(5)$, $m^{*}(r_{s}=5)=0.74(1)$, and $m^{*}(r_{s}=10)=0.70(3)$, which show a clear decrease (suppression) of the effective mass as the density is lowered (i.e., as $r_{s}$ increases); from these numerical results conclude that for the fully spin-polarized (ferromagnetic) 2D HEG the effective mass is suppressed as density is lowered. 
Fig. 7

Note the comparison with other theoretical and experimental results: the paramagnetic QMC results presented here do not show the enhancement at low density predicted by some GW calculations or suggested by some experiments, whereas the ferromagnetic QMC results showing suppression of $m^{*}$ with decreasing density are in qualitative agreement with certain experimental measurements on spin-polarized 2D systems (for example the experimental results of Padmanabhan et al.). 
[1]

State the overall physical conclusion: the extrapolated DMC results imply distinct density dependences of the quasiparticle effective mass depending on spin polarization — paramagnetic 2D HEGs have $m^{*}\approx 1$ across $r_{s}=1$--$10$ with no significant low-density enhancement, while ferromagnetic 2D HEGs exhibit a suppressed $m^{*}$ as density is lowered, with the numerical examples given above illustrating this trend.

motivating_questions.content: The sign and magnitude of interaction-induced renormalization of the electron mass in 2D HEGs is of both conceptual and practical importance: a strong enhancement of $m^{*}$ at low density would signal increasingly strong correlation effects modifying quasiparticle dynamics, whereas suppression of $m^{*}$ with spin polarization influences transport and spintronics properties. Prior theoretical and experimental studies offered conflicting indications—GW calculations and some experiments suggested a paramagnetic low-density enhancement, while other theoretical treatments and measurements disagreed. Without precise thermodynamic-limit benchmarks, it was unclear which trends are intrinsic to the homogeneous 2D electron liquid and which are artifacts of approximations or finite-size effects. Delivering extrapolated $m^{*}$ values and their spin dependence therefore clarifies the physical behavior of the interacting 2D Fermi liquid in the density range studied.
